content.text: In a village nestled between jagged mountains and a forest so dense the sunlight never quite reached the ground, there lived a poor woodcutter and his son.
The boy was clever, quick with his hands, and had a curiosity that often led him deeper into the woods than his father allowed.

One evening, as the wind howled through the trees like a chorus of lost souls, the woodcutter warned his son never to stray beyond the old oak—the one with the gnarled roots that twisted like serpents beneath the earth.
"There are things in those woods that should not be disturbed," he said, his voice low and grave.
The boy nodded, but the warning only made the forbidden path more tempting.
The next morning, the boy rose before dawn and slipped into the forest.
The air was thick with mist, and the trees seemed to lean in closer as he passed, their branches creaking like old bones.
He reached the old oak and pressed onward, his boots sinking into the damp earth.
The deeper he went, the colder it became, until his breath fogged in front of him.
Then, beneath the roots of a fallen tree, he saw it—a small glass bottle, half-buried in the dirt, its surface clouded with grime.
It was no larger than his palm, but something about it made his skin prickle.
He brushed away the dirt, and the moment his fingers touched the glass, a faint blue light flickered inside.
His pulse quickened. Carefully, he pulled the cork free.
A hiss escaped, like steam from a kettle, and then—a voice.
Not a voice from this world, but something ancient and hungry, slithering into his ears like smoke.
"Free me," it whispered, "and I will make you rich beyond measure."  The boy stumbled back, but the voice only grew louder, twisting into a laugh that echoed inside his skull.
"You have no choice," it hissed. "I am bound to this bottle, and you have set me loose.
Now, you will serve me—or I will take what is mine."  A shadow stretched from the bottle, long and thin, like a finger reaching for him.
The boy’s breath came in ragged gasps.
He had heard the tales—how spirits trapped in bottles demanded payment, how they twisted wishes into curses, how they devoured the souls of those foolish enough to free them.
But he was clever, and he had one last trick.
"I will free you," the boy said, his voice steady despite the terror clawing at his throat, "but first, you must prove you can grant wishes.
Show me a sign."  The shadow hesitated. Then, with a sound like cracking ice, the bottle trembled.
A single gold coin materialized in the boy’s palm, warm and heavy. The spirit’s voice dripped with amusement. "See?
I keep my promises. Now, free me."  The boy closed his fist around the coin. "Very well," he said.
"But first, tell me—what is your name?"  The shadow recoiled. "Names have power," it snarled.
"You know this."  "Then I will not free you until you tell me."  The spirit let out a shriek that shook the trees, and for a moment, the boy thought it would tear him apart.
But then, in a voice like grinding teeth, it hissed, "Fine.
I am—"  The boy didn’t wait for it to finish.
He jammed the cork back into the bottle and hurled it as far as he could into the undergrowth.
The moment it left his hand, the forest fell silent. The mist retreated. The trees stood still.
The boy’s heart pounded as he turned and ran, his boots pounding against the earth, until he burst back into the clearing where his father waited, his face pale with worry.
The woodcutter grabbed his son’s shoulders. "What happened? You’re shaking."  The boy opened his palm.
The gold coin gleamed in the morning light. His father’s eyes widened, but the boy only smiled.
"I found something in the woods," he said.

"Something that will change our lives."  That night, as the fire crackled in the hearth, the boy told his father everything—how he had outwitted the spirit, how he had tricked it into revealing its name before sealing it away forever.
The woodcutter listened in silence, his expression grave.
When the boy finished, his father placed a hand on his shoulder. "You were lucky," he said.
"But luck runs out. Promise me you’ll never go back to those woods."  The boy nodded.
He had no desire to return. Years passed.
The gold coin became many, and the woodcutter’s son grew into a man of means, respected in the village.

But sometimes, on the coldest nights when the wind howled like a thing alive, he would wake in a sweat, certain he had heard a whisper from the forest—his name, spoken in a voice that was not quite human.
Each time, he would sit up, reach beneath his pillow, and feel the weight of the bottle’s cork, still tucked safely away.
And each time, the fear would fade, replaced by the quiet certainty that the spirit was still trapped, still bound, still powerless.
And so, the man lived a long and happy life, his days filled with warmth and light, his nights undisturbed by shadows.
The forest remained silent, the bottle buried deep, and the secret of the spirit’s name known only to him.
And though the wind still carried whispers, they were only the wind—nothing more.
